sentences.text: That's right.
So, actually I did not have any non-Jewish friends in Riga, because we didn't have any mutual interests.
My dream was to finish my education and to go to Palestine.
And this feeling I couldn't share with any goy.
In 1940, Latvia was occupied by the Russian forces, which meant a great change in our everyday life, as well.
People were scared.
We didn't know what was going to happen to us.
People had already been arrested.
People were driven out of their homes.
I don't mean Jewish people, in particular-- in general, the population.
As I've mentioned before, I used to go at five or six weeks home to be with my family.
On one occasion, there was a ball, so-called ball, in a club.
And which I didn't want to go at all.
But luckily, I had an-- had had an argument with a Jewish boy who was our neighbor.
His name was Orele.
And my mother thought, since we are so on such friendly terms with the family, this should be made up, and we should be friends again.
And I wanted to make my mother happy, and I went with Orele to that ball.
And--
His--
Aaron Orele.
O-R-E-L-E.
Gottschalk is his-- was his surname.
He was also killed.
Well, we were dancing, and all of a sudden, a tall gentleman, wearing a Russian uniform-- an officer's uniform-- came up to me and asked Orele if I could-- if he could dance with me.
I didn't want it.
But Orele said, that's all right.

And I realized immediately that this man was not a Russian, that he was wearing already the new Russian uniform, that actually they were Latvians who had just finished military academy, and were already there dressed in Russian uniform.
He was dancing.
He was a very, very, very thoughtful man.
And the only thing he asked me was whether I live in Goldingen, to which I answered, no I did not.
I live in Riga.
And then, he asked me what street, and I just mentioned the number of the house, but not the apartment.
And he didn't know my surname, either.
Stabu iela.
S-T-A-B-U.
Six.
Yes, but you know that we were forced to leave-- to leave our real and beautiful apartment, because the Russians came in, and they needed housing for their families.
So they threw all, and within 24 hours, people had to leave their homes, out into the street.
And people were without a shelter.
So our dressmaker did us a favor, and she offered me her-- a room in her apartment.
So that was Stabu iela.
The rest that is happening is happening in Stabu iela.
So when he came the second time to ask me to dance with him, and I was not happy about that either, he asked me if he could take me home.
I said, yes, but under one condition-- the three of us, Orele, who brought me to this place, and you.
He said, all right, and the two of them accompanied me home.
And that was all.
So he knew where I lived in Riga.
Several months passed, and my sister had come to Riga to visit me.
On the railroad platform, we saw this very man approaching us.
But he didn't notice us.
And I said to my sister, let's run away-- I don't want to see him.
And this we did.
So I didn't have a chance to speak him, because I didn't want to.
And now, the rest comes when what happens when the Germans occupy Riga.
So, are there any other questions?
Can you describe the whole atmosphere in Riga just before the war broke out?
I mean, before 22nd of June, of '41, from political point of view?
What did you know what's going on in Poland?
What did you know about the army, the German army movements, et cetera?
Yes, actually, we knew most things.
German Jews tried to find refuge in Latvia.
And by Ulmanis, our Prime Minister, they were really given the right, asylum, in this country.
Most of them were students from Vienna and Germany.
Polish officers came, also-- fled to Latvia.
But they were interned in some camps.
So we knew there was unrest, and there was danger over the border.
The Russians-- the Russians exiled thousands of families, Jewish and non-Jewish, without any reason, to Siberia, because they thought they were bourgeois, and they didn't want them.
The Russians, the Russian officers, mainly, as I have mentioned before, drove people out of their homes.
Their women were clad in night gowns, as in ball-- as in going-- as if going to a ball, but they didn't understand it's a night gown, with long, white gloves, walking along the streets of Riga.
It was a terrible sight.
They robbed all the shops where watches were sold, because they couldn't get watches in Russia.
And it made a terrible impression on all the population.
We knew something is going to happen.
Socially, so they said there would be Jewish schools.
It means, what is important for me?
So, I was convinced by a friend of mine in Goldingen to give up my studies at the English college.
And in order to help Jewish people, to bring up their children in a Jewish spirit and way, to attend some Jewish courses in Riga-- Yiddish courses.
And that there would be Jewish schools.
And I attended such a course, which lasted three months.
And to our great regret, we found out that it was all a big lie.
There were no Jewish schools at all.
There were only Russian and Latvian schools.
So, I found it absolutely appalling and disgusting because there was a new subject.
Lenin and Stalin were quoted everywhere.
It was mandatory to learn everything about the Great October Revolution.
Well-known local and international authors were forbidden.
Even a book like which is not anti-communist, was forbidden.
So we lived in a constant horror-- what is going to be forbidden in future, and who is going to be exiled in future?
Many Jewish people who were not millionaires were exiled for their ideology.
Either [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH].
Among them, very many acquaintances of mine.
I even had made a list of those here, in Bat Yam, a list of those people who were exiled.
And many of them didn't return.
They perished in the woods, in the jungle there.
Some of them returned and are in Israel now.
So, life for the Jewish people became very bad.
We had the feeling of the time, actually, before the war.
But we knew that-- and we were absolutely, extremely poor then-- we knew we needed money to get a so-called certificate, which we couldn't get without any money.
So my brother was engaged to a young lady who was the daughter of wealthy parents.
She succeeded in coming over in the late '30s to Palestine.
And he remained, and wanted to wait a couple of years until he'll make some money in order to join her.
Unfortunately, he failed.
And here we come to '41, June, '41, and your parents come to join you with your sister to Riga.
Where do they live?
Where do you live, all of them?
We all live in that small room which I mentioned before.
Well, everything was very sad, because the time had begun when Jewish people could not get any food.
It means-- if Latvians saw a Jew, or a Jew was standing, queuing up for food, they were just dragged away.

But since I looked in my early-- in my young days-- non-Jewish, and spoke the language very well, so I made the purchases for all the family and even for the neighbors, because in our house, there were some neighbors who were later killed, too.
No.
There was no work at all.
Jewish people were not supposed to work-- only forced labor given by the Germans.
Yes.
And the war broke out.
Yes.
I tried, as much as I could, to defend my family in all respects.
My family means my parents and my little sister.

Since I was the older one and rather energetic, and I had got used to being on my own, I decided to go out into the street in the first place-- which was very dangerous on the first of July, when the Russian troops occupied the city-- Jewish people were hiding, and I wanted to see what was going on.
I wouldn't do it today.
So I saw Latvian women throwing flowers at the occupying German forces, singing songs, being dressed in their national dresses.
It was a great festivity in the country.
So they thought they had got rid of the Russians, and now they were welcoming their guests.
And here I have to interrupt saying, what I read in a German newspaper two years, three years later.
The name of the article is, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
But it means, we know our friends, in inverted commas.
The Latvians were a people who always welcomed those-- the winners, and always destroyed the losers.
And then they enumerated it.
So the Germans realized that they had used the Latvians for the dirty job, and now they don't need them.
And they spat into their face, saying, we know what you are worth.
And that was the truth.
Oh, no, no, no.
The night prior to the occupation, we were all sitting in the cellar.
They were bombing the city.
We knew that the German-- the German forces are not far away.
We could hear the city being bombed, even in the cellar.
And really, they destroyed part of the old city.
And the next morning, we heard already people so we went out of the cellar, we were told to get out of there because the Germans are in town.
So, that's it.
Then, on the very first day of the Russian occupation, I heard it with my own eyes-- over the radio.
The first day of the German occupation, the Latvian Colonel Veiss, who was a boss of the Latvian police, made the speaker on the radio pronounce, I could say it in Latvian, hello, hello, Latvia.
[SPEAKING LATVIAN] These were the cities where there are radio stations.

He suggested every, every Latvian who still has weapons to gather at the [NON-ENGLISH], picture is here, at the [NON-ENGLISH] which is in the old city of Riga, in the struggle against the local and the foreign enemy.
The local enemy are the Jews and the communists-- this is what he said-- and the outsiders are the Russians.
And thousands of Latvians came with weapons, which they had hidden during the year of the Russian occupation.
And there, they were mobilized, organized, for the first struggle, in inverted commas, against the Jews and communists.
I remember and will never forget that very first night.
My nanny was staying with us.
She had come from the countryside in order to help us somehow, some way.
Yes, that's right.
The same room.
She had-- she had been living with our family for over 33 years, so she was as a member of the family.
We heard footsteps at night, knocking on doors.
And there were Jewish-- it so happens there were Jewish families living there, the Kotten and the Joffer and us.
So they knocked on our door.
And my nanny went out, and she said, what is your wish?
What do you want?
We have come to pick up Jews.
She said, there are no Jews living in this apartment.
And she closed the door.
And she saw that she's Latvian.
But upstairs, there was the Kotten family.
So I heard them-- I overheard them, or I eavesdropped-- Kotten, Kotten Is it a Latvian name, or is it a Hebrew, a Jewish name?
And then they decided to make the people come out, and the people never returned.
The same happened to Mrs.
Joffer, who lived the same floor, her husband.
Only males were taken away.
Women were not arrested that night.
So--
The 1st of July.
The night of the 1st of July.
So, the Germans had not started their job, yet.
The Latvians were given full command of the people, of the Jewish people, I mean.
They arrested about 10,000 males-- among them my uncle Herman and his son, Robert.
And they never saw the daylight again.
No, not my father, because my nanny said there are no males.
By the way, my father looked so goyish, when people saw him, they took him for a Latvian.
And he spoke Latvian also without an accent.
They wouldn't have arrested him, I'm sure.
So, what happened next?
They arrested 10,000 men.
And then the Germans took over.
And then there was already a Reichskommissariat, and the Gebietskommissariat, and all the rest.
And the Jewish people were taken to different places, headquarters of the police, to the Perkonkrusts.
They were taken-- and me too, I mean-- to different places where there were-- where Latvian policemen were working.
And they made the Jewish people do different odd jobs.
By odd jobs, I mean scrubbing floors, and cleaning, and carrying furniture, et cetera, et cetera.
But we never knew, when they took us in the morning, to the police headquarters, we never knew whether we would return.
Because now and again girls, particularly, were disappearing.
So, later we heard they were raped and shot dead.
After the 1st of July, I believe it was a day or two, we heard a German yell in our courtyard, Stabu iela six.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] So we-- I was the one, again, who went to face this man.
And he had us lined up, and made us follow him.
So in the next, in the close neighborhood, there was a very beautiful building where they had driven people out, already.
And they occupied there a beautiful apartment.
In each apartment, there was a German officer living without his family-- because their families, unlike the Russians, stayed in Germany.
And the next morning, so I was standing in a line, and he said, our boss will come, and he will pick out a servant for him.
And I felt intuition-- I felt that he's going to pick me out.
I don't know why, I can't explain it.
I think so.
His name was Goedde.
Yes.
G-O-E-D-D-E, Oberleutnant.
And he was inspecting the girls.
We were standing in two lines.
And I was-- I was expecting him to say, you are the one.
And this is what he said.
I was not afraid of him.
I don't know why.
I didn't-- I didn't have the feeling of fright, because he seemed to be a very peaceful man.
And I was right.
He was not in the SS.
And so I was his servant.
I had to clean up, et cetera.
He didn't give me especial difficult work.
One evening, he said, ask all your girlfriends who live in the other apartments, to make together with you, under your leadership, a beautiful supper.
Supper means small sandwiches.
Well, we did our very best, because there were some girls who came from well-to-do families, and this was not, obviously the first time that they were setting a table.
And then, we went-- we went home.
On the next morning I came to work, I saw all the sandwiches were on the table, not touched.
So he came up to me, and he apologized.
He said, all my friends were German officers, and they are forbidden to eat anything made by Jewish people.
So, the following day, somebody rang the doorbell.
I was the one to answer the bell at the door.
And my colleague, a non-Jewish lady from the English college, came to sleep with Mr.
Goedde.
So you see, things happened in those days too.
And like this, you go on as a [GERMAN] of Mr.--
Goedde, for a very short time.
And later, I was transferred to Irwin Henkleman's place, who turned out to be a wonderful person from Hanover.
And he told me, he predicted Hitler's end, and he said that this can't go on for very long, because he's a-- it's a terrible regime.
And that he is a despot, et cetera, et cetera.
He could have been killed for it.
It means he trusted me.
And he told me that his aunt, as far as I remember, was Jewish.
So, now and again, he gave us money, some Deutsch Mark, Ostmark, and some food or some cigarettes for those girls who were smoking.
And he kept us from cleaning too much.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] He just wanted to look through the window, not to-- It shouldn't be too clean.
He was sorry for us.
And he wrote down, in order to go out, to go home, I needed a document.
He wrote down, Fraulein Zelma Shipsolovich.
I said, Mr.
Henkleman, this is not valid.
It is die Juden Zelma Shipsol-- He said, I am not able to do that.
I can't write it down.
So he was a fine man.
So, you were working with him from morning till evening, and you went back home to sleep with your family.
Yes, that was in the next town, next door almost-- of course.
Yes.
And for that time, you were not harassed, and you were--
I was.
One day, for instance, we couldn't go to that work.
We were taken to the police station in Squallatile Street, Squalla Street.
We were in a crowded room.
Men, young, young-- everybody was young.
Young men and women.
And we didn't know what they were going to do, because we had heard about the killings already.
So we were sitting there without any bread and without any water all day.
Some girls were dragged away that didn't come back.
I still remember the name of one of those Latvians.
And then, they took us to the old city-- in the future, I mean-- they took us to the old city of Riga, to clean up the mess.
You know, the city had been partly destroyed.
So we, young and old, we were obliged to carry stones from one place to another, which didn't make sense, because we couldn't carry heavy stones.
They just wanted to make us work.
And here, perhaps, I should mention that the guards were all Latvians, very young, from different Latvian nationalist organizations.
And I saw a Jewish lady, a dentist, who was our neighbor.
I don't remember her name.
Or I believe I never knew her-- knew her name.
She collapsed because it was a very hot, July day, and I felt terrible.
So that young man, I don't remember the organization he belonged to, came up to me and said-- and this is what later happened now and again to me-- oh, you are not Jewish.
You're making fun.
It's either your mother or father who is Jewish.
And you can get a Latvian passport in Gondinger. said, if this old lady can carry stones in this hot July sun, I can, too.
I am 100% Jewish.
My father is a Jew, and my mother is a Jewess.
So, this is what they sometimes tried to do, to pick out girls whom they liked, and perhaps temporarily, I don't know, and not provide a passport to live in town, et cetera.
Were you called to the prefecture?
Yes, I was not called.
One of the days, when we were working in the old city of Riga, they took us for a change to a different place.
And that was one of the horror places, like the Perkonkrust.
That was the police headquarters in Riga, in the center of the city.
There we were also asked to clean.
Women were asked to clean.
The men disappeared.
We didn't know where.
And while I was cleaning the window, I saw terrible sights.
In the suburbs of Riga, there was very many rich Jews left, rich and intelligentsia of the Jewish people.
Some of them were dragged to the courtyard.
They were beaten to death.
They were lying in their pool of blood, which I saw.
And then they were--they spilled cold water on them in order to revive them, and to beat them again.
And the terrible shrieks and yelling from all the parts of the prefectura.
And unfortunately, the Russian prisoners of war shared our fate.
They were also sitting there and eating their crust of black bread.
I don't know what happened to them, but we were there together.
This-- there I saw a teacher, a former teacher of mine, even, one of the torturers.
And they were all young Latvians.
There was no German there.
Most of the people who were there did not see sunlight again.
I can't tell you the exact number, but less than a week.
Nothing.
I was just cleaning and washing.
And nobody touched me.
And nobody called me names.
But I was terribly frightened-- not only to me, to many of us.
We're in the same position.
We didn't know what's going to happen, whether they're going to kill us, to rape us, or what.
Mm, just can't tell you, a group of about 15 to 20, always about the same.
Not released.
I mean from the prefecture.
Sent home.
Sent home.
Also in groups.
Also in groups.
And then, the next-- so then, as the newspapers were full of anti-Semitic articles, the Nuremberg laws were also already imposed on the Jewish people.
We had no right to use the sidewalk.
No right to buy anything, no food, nothing.
No seat at the cinemas, et cetera.
Not using the tram cars, the buses.
And wearing the yellow patch on the breast and on the back.
And they started opening the ghetto.
So, the ghetto was closed on the 25th of October.

But Jewish people, like my family and others, were settled their much earlier, sooner, because the old-- the place where they established the ghetto had been formerly inhabited by very poor Russian people.
And these poor Russian people, against their will, of course, were taken, thrown over to Riga, to the beautiful Jewish houses.
And the Jewish people had to settle in the ghetto.
So, we had to go rather early-- I don't remember now the exact date, but it was early.
You were arrested in the Perkonkrust headquarters.
Yes.
That was also in July.
On one of these days, when groups of young women were taken to work or somewhere, we were driven in an unknown direction, but we knew what street we were in.
And we saw we were approaching-- we were approaching the house of the former Jewish banker, Schmulian.
And we knew there was a Perkonkrust, and we knew that would be our end, because we have never heard of anyone return from the perfecture.
Some people had returned, but never from the Perkonkrust.
And they usually arrested young women there, not males, very few males, only for hard work.
So, they drove us along Valdemara Street to number 19.
We entered a gate.
We entered the courtyard.
And there was a table was placed there.
And the Perkonkrust officers were rushing and doing things, very, very, very restless.
So a new group was brought in, and they were already eager to get the jewelry.
I had nothing, but there were some Jewish ladies who did have jewelry.
So they wrote down, they knew exactly, that the Jewish ladies would never see the jewelry again.
But they wrote it down proforma, as if they confiscated it temporarily, and so on.
All right.
